content.full_text:
Re
Fern Polrrrcar PnecrrcEs CorraMrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
November 18,2002
Ms. BarbaraZ.Leibold
Van Blarcom, Leibold, McClendon
& Mann
23422 Mill Creek Drive, Suite 105
Laguna Hills, CA 92653
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-218
Dear Ms. Leibold
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Thomas Buckley,
Councilmember for the City of Lake Elsinore, regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r The Fair Political Practices
Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice; this
advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us
are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) l
FPPC Ops. 71; Govt. Code section 83114.)
QUESTIONS
1. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifying him
from serving as the City's representative on the Riverside County Transportation
commission ("Transportation Commission"), the western Riverside Council of
Govemments ('tylBgG"), of various subcommittees of these entities?
2. Generally, does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest
disqualiffing him from being involved in City Council decisions which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on clients of O'Reilly Public Relations
("O'Reilly"), a media consulting company?
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. A-02-218
Page No. 2
3. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from being involved in decisions of the Lake Elsinore City Council ("City Council")
concerning a pending lawsuit, and settlement of that suit, between the City of Lake
Elsinore (the "City") and the Elsinore Valley Municipal Water District ("Water
District")?
4. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualiffing him
being involved in decisions of the City Council concerning City funding for the Lake
Elsinore & San Jacinto Watershed Authority ("Watershed Authority'), the prioritizing of
funding and scheduling of projects for the Watershed Authority, and involving the
Watershed Authority as aparty to the Water District settlement?
5. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifying him
from being involved in decisions of the City Council concerning the Riverside County
Integrated Plan ("County Plan")
CONCLUSIONS
1. No. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to a public
official when making, participating in making, or influencing agovernmental decision.
These provisions do not disqualify an official from holding an elected or appointed public
office. Other laws, however, may apply.
2. No. Generally, Councilmember Buckley will not have conflict of interest
disqualifizing him from being involved in City Council decisions which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on clients of O'Reilly, since those clients
will not be considered sources of income to him. (Section 82030(a).) However, if a City
Council decision involving one or more of those clients will also have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on O'Reilly, he will have a potentially disqualiffing
conflict of interest.
3. It appears that Councilmember Buckley will have a conflict of interest
potentially disquali$ing him from participating in City Council decisions concerning the
lawsuit with the Water District; it appears reasonably foreseeable that that these decisions
may have a material financial effect upon O'Rellly, a source of income to
Councilmember Buckley. This is a factual question for Councilmember Buckley to
ultimately decide.
4. & 5. No. It does not appear that Councilmember Buckley will have a conflict
of interest potentially disqualifuing him from participating in City Council decisions
concerning City funding and prqect scheduling for the Watershed Authority, and City
Council decisions involving the County Plan. Based on your facts, it does not appear
reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material financial effect upon
O'Reilly.


File No. A-02-218
Page No. 3
FACTS
O' Reilly/The Buckley Company
In January of 2002, Councilmember Buckley resigned his salaried employment2
with O'Reilly and formed his own media relations agency, The Buckley Company, which
he operates as a sole proprietorship. The Buckley Company has a number of clients,
including developers, but the majority of its income is currently derived from
subcontracted services performed on behalf of O'Reilly for several of O'Reilly's clients.3
During the past twelve months, O'Reilly's clients included the Water District, the
Watershed Authority, and Riverside County. O'Reilly's contract with the Water District
has expired, but is anticipated to be renewed. O'Reilly's contracts with the Watershed
Authority and Riverside County are ongoing.
As an employee of O'Reilly, Councilmember Buckley provided media relations
services to the Water District and Riverside County during the past twelve months.
Although the Water District, Riverside County, and the Watershed Authority are not
clients of The Buckley Company, The Buckley Company has performed media relations
services during the past twelve months, as a subcontractor on O'Reilly's behalf, for
Riverside County.
Local Government Entittes
In addition to his duties as a City Council member, Councilmember Buckley is
also an alternate representative, on behalf of the City, to the Transportation Commission.
Representatives to the Transportation Commission are paid a stipend of $100 per meeting
they attend. To date, Councilmember Buckley has not attended a meeting of the
Transportation Commission. He also serves as the City's representative to WRCG. As
the city's representative, he is paid a stipend of $50 per meeting attended.
Pending Activities
The City has a pending lawsuit against the Water District concerning alleged
illegal diversions of water from the San Jacinto River, which feeds into Lake Elsinore.
Settlement negotiations have been ongoing and it is possible that a settlement of this suit
will be submitted to the City Council for approval. Among the provisions being
considered is permanent use of recycled water to supplement nafural flows of San Jacinto
River water into Lake Elsinore. It is expected that the eventual resolution of this suit will
have a significant financial impact on the City and Water District. In the past, O'Reilly
was hired by the Water District to conduct a public awareness campaign concerning use
of recycled water in Lake Elsinore. If the Water District renews its public awareness
program, it is anticipated that O'Reilly will be retained to conduct that program.
2 Upon assuming office, Councilmember Buckley reported that his annual salary from O'Reilly
was befween $10,001 to $100,000.
3 You state that the cumulative arurual income to The Buckley Company from O'Reilly is
projected to be in excess of$20,000.


File No. A-02-218
Page No. 4
The City, the Water District, Riverside County, and two other public entities
together comprise the membership of the Watershed Authority. The Watershed
Authority was formed to enhance water quality in Lake Elsinore and the San Jacinto
River watershed. The City, on occasion, has allocated funding to the Watershed
Authority in the form of advances that are to be reimbursed by the Watershed Authority
The Watershed Authority also receives funding from the State of Califomia. The City
Council helps make decisions concerning the projects undertaken by the Watershed
Authority,.including future projects that would be part of the settlement, above, if the
settlement is subsequently approved. It is also suggested that the Watershed Authorify
assume a role akin to a mediator or administrator in connection with this settlement.
The Transportation Commission is a forum within Riverside County allowing
local officials to participate in, and influence, transportation planning and programming
within the county. In addition, the Transportation Commission plans and funds specifii
transportation projects with the county. Membership includes the Riverside County
Board of Supervisors, one representative from each city in Riverside County, and an
individual appointed by the Governor.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from makin g, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. Section 87100 does not prohibit a public official from
holding a position in an agency, but describes those circumstances in which a public
official may not be involved in the governmental decisions of his or her agency.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifiiing conflict of interest (regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(l) -
(8)), which is discussed below. The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest
may occur whenever a public official makes, participates in making, or influences a
govemmental decision which may materially affect one or more of his or her economic
interests.
l'. & 2. rs councilmember Buckley a public official making, participating in
making or influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As an elected
member of the City of Lake Elsinore City Council, he is a public official. (Section 82048;
regulation 18701(a).) As a council member, unless disqualified under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act, he will make, participate in making, and influence the
governmental decisions in question. (Section 87100; regulations 18702.1 - 15702.3.)


File No. A-02-218
Page No. 5
3. What are Councilmember Buckley's economic interests?
The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined in
regulations 18703-18703.5. Under the facts provided, Councilmember Buckley's
potential economic interests, which might give rise to a conflict of interest, are The
Buckley Company, O'Reilly, and other sources of income to The Buckley Company
aggregating $500 or more over the twelve-month period preceding his making,
participating in making, or influencing govemmental decisions in the matters discussed
above.a
The Buckley Company
The Buckley Company is an economic interest to him, as a business entity for
which he is the sole owner. (Regulation 18703.1(b).) Although you do not state the
value of his direct investment in his business, if this value is $2,000 or more, his direct
investment (as a matter independent of his ownership interest) would be yet another basis
upon which to identiff The Buckley Company as a business entity that is an economic
interest to him. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.1(a).) Finally, he also has an economic
interest in The Buckley Company as a source of income to him that aggregates to $500 or
more over a twelve-month period prior to the date that he will be called upon to make,
participate in making, or influence the govemmental decisions you describe. (Section
871 03(c); regulation 1 8703.3.)
O'Reilly
O'Reilly, as his former employer during the past twelve months, is also an
economic interest to Councilmember Buckley since it is a source of income to him
personally, aggregating $500 or more over the prior twelve months.s
Moreover, in some circumstances, a public official may also have an economic
interest in clients or other sources of income to a business entity in which he or she has an
ownership interest. (Section 82030(a).) The most corlmon case is when the public
official's ownership interest in the business entity is lO-percent or greater. (/d.) Since
Councilmember Buckley's ownership interest in The Buckley Company is 1O-percent or
greater, clients such as O'Reilly and other sources of income to The Buckley Company
a In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period.
(Regulations 1 8703.5 and 18705.5.)
t Whe., a source of income is a former employer the former employer is not considered a source of
income to the public official if all of the following apply: l) all income from the employer was received by
or accrued to the public official prior to the time he or she became a public official; 2) the income was
received in the normal course of the previous employment; and 3) there was no expectation by the public
official at the time he or she assumed office of renewed employment with the former employer. (Regulation
18703.3(b).) In a telephone conversation with the Commission's staff on November 5,2002, you indicated
that Councilmember Buckley was sworn into office on November 27,2001. Since his employment with
O'Reilly did not terminate until January 2002, this exception is not applicable.


File No. A-02-218
Page No. 6
aggregating $500 or more over a relevant twelve-month period are also economic
interests to him under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Murad Advice
Letter, No. I-02-100.)
Riverside County, Water District, Watershed Authority
Since neither Councilmember Buckley nor The Buckley Company have an
ownership interest in O'Reilly, clients of O'Reilly who are to O'Reilly sources of income
are not considered to be sources of income to Councilmember Buckley or The Buckley
Company.6
4. Will these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A person, including a source of income, in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if that person, either
directly or by an agent, initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or
similar request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation l870a.l@)(1) - (a)(2).)
A person is the subject of a proceeding conceming the decision before the agency if the
"decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with," that person. (Regulation
l87oa.1@)(2).) If a person who is an economic interest to a public official is not directly
involved in a governmental decision, the person is deemed to be indirectly involved.
(Regulation 187 04.1 (b).)
The Buckley Company and O'Reilly are not the initiators or subjects of or named
parties in, or otherwise meet the criteria to be considered directly involved in City
Council decisions concerning the Water District lawsuit, funding and scheduling of
projects for the Watershed Authority, and decisions involving the County Plan. The two
companies, therefore, are deemed to be indirectly involved in City Council decisions
concerning these matters.
5. Wilt the financial affect on one or more of Councilmember Buckley's economic
interests be material?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
goverTrmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material . (Regulation
18700(a).) For indirectly involved business entities such as The Buckley Company, and
indirectly involved sources of income such as the O'Reilly (and other clients of The
Buckley Company) which are not publicly haded or eligible to be publicly traded, and
are of a small financial size, the financial effect of a governmental decision is material if
the decision will result in:
6In addition, these local govemment entities that are clients of, and sources of income to, O'Reilly
are not parents or subsidiaries of, nor business entities otherwise related to, O'Reilly. Thus, they would not
be economic interests to Councilmember Buckley due to a business affiliation. (Regulation 18703.1(c).)


File No. A-02-218
Page No. 7
(a) an increase or decrease in the business entity's or source of income's gross
revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more;
(b) the business entity or source of income incurring or avoiding additional
expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or
(c) an increase or decrease in the value of the business entity's or source of
income's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a)(A)-(c).)
This is afactual question and there are no facts before us to indicate whether an
anticipated financial effect would be material, under the standards above. Ultimately,
this is a question for Councilmember Buckley, and not us, to decide. The Commission
does not act as a finder of fact when it offers advice. (Regulation l13z9(b)(7).)
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that these governmental decisions wiII result in one or
more of the materiality standards being met?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the decision has a reasonably
foreseeable impact on a public official's economic interests that is material . (Regulation
18700(a).) A material financial effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably
foreseeable if there is a substantial probability, under the totality of the circumstances,
that it will occur. (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner, supra fciting t].5. v. Mtsstssippi
Valley Generattng Co.,364 U.S. 520, 555 (1961)1.) In this instance, the circumstances
are comparable to several of the scenarios examined in In re Thorner and lead to similar
advice regarding the reasonable foreseeability of a financial effect.
Water District
Settlement of the litigation between the City and the Water District, as currently
negotiated, would involve permanent use of recycled water to supplement natural flows
of river water into Lake Elsinore. In the past, the Water District has implemented interim
flows of recycled water into Lake Elsinore and in conjunction with such activity,
sponsored a public awareness campaign concerning use of recycled water in Lake
Elsinore. The Water District retained O'Reilly to conduct that campaign. You state that
if the Water District decides to sponsor a future public awareness campaign concerning
use of recycled water, it is anticipated that O'Reilly will conduct that campaign as well.
This is analogous to a situation discussed in In re Thorner, in.which the past
business dealings of the parties created a substantial probability that a particular agency
decision would have a material financial effect upon the parties, even in the absence of a
current formal agreement or understanding between the parties. Here, the past linkage
between the Water District's introduction of re-cycled water into Lake Elsinore and its
sponsorship of a public awareness campaign concerning use of re-cycled water, together
with hiring O'Reilly to conduct that campaign, creates a substantial probability that a
settlement affecting permanent use of re-cycled water in Lake Elsinore will similarly
result in a public awareness campaign sponsored by the Water District, and that O'Reilly
will conduct that campaign. Indeed, you concede in your letter that if such a campaign is


File No. A-02-218
Page No. 8
conducted, it is anticipated that O'Reilly will conduct that campaign. Thus, unless
Councilmember Buckley is able to acquire facts demonstrating that the Water District
will not conduct this public awareness campaign, it appears reasonably foreseeable that
O'Reilly will be financially affectedT by a City Council decision regarding settlement of
the lawsuit. Whether the financial effect will be material as discussed under Step 5 above
is a factual question the Councilmember must ultimately decide.
Waters hed Authority/Riverstde County
In re Thorner also addressed a sifuation where aparly currently doing business
with a public official has submitted, or will submit, a bid on a project allowed by the
official's govefilmental decision. Unless thatparty is awarded a contract for that project,
there is an extra degree of remoteness added to the foreseeability of a financial effect
flowing from the earlier decision; the contract has to be awarded and the party has to
fiilize his existing business arrangement with the official to provide services called for
under the contract before the decision would have a financial effect. The Commission
advised that in such context, a financial effect was not reasonably foreseeable.
This is analogous to the upcoming City Council decisions regarding the
Watershed Authority and Riverside County. Both entities have current contracts with
O'Reilly to perform government, media or community relations services. However, you
provide no facts indicating that the upcoming decisions, which concern funding and
prioritizing ofprojects for the Watershed Authority, and Riverside County's County Plan,
have any relationship to whether these entities might seek additional governmental,
media, or community relations services from O'Reilly. Once those decisions are made,
the Watershed Authority and Riverside County would then have to not only implement
these decisions, but also (1) undertake some form of governmental, media, or community
relations effort as part of that implementation, and (2) ttilize their existing contracts with
O'Reilly to do so, before the City Council decisions would have a financial effect on
O'Reilly. This demonstrates that extra degree of remoteness spoken of in In re Thorner
as characteizing a decision that will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect.
Thus, it does not appear reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material
financial effect upon O'Reilly.
7. & 8. Public Generallyllegally Required Participation
We have not gone on to analyze these last two steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies when the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the public
official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
Step eight is an exception that applies when the public official is legally required to
participate in the decision. You have not provided any facts to suggest that these
exceptions apply.
' we disc.rss further below whether that financial effect is to be deemed material.


File No. A-02-218
Page No. 9
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
L.
Counsel, Legal Division
KGje

sections.question:
1. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifying him
from serving as the City's representative on the Riverside County Transportation
commission ("Transportation Commission"), the western Riverside Council of
Govemments ('tylBgG"), of various subcommittees of these entities?
2. Generally, does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest
disqualiffing him from being involved in City Council decisions which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on clients of O'Reilly Public Relations
("O'Reilly"), a media consulting company?
3. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from being involved in decisions of the Lake Elsinore City Council ("City Council")
concerning a pending lawsuit, and settlement of that suit, between the City of Lake
Elsinore (the "City") and the Elsinore Valley Municipal Water District ("Water
District")?
4. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualiffing him
being involved in decisions of the City Council concerning City funding for the Lake
Elsinore & San Jacinto Watershed Authority ("Watershed Authority'), the prioritizing of
funding and scheduling of projects for the Watershed Authority, and involving the
Watershed Authority as aparty to the Water District settlement?
5. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifying him
from being involved in decisions of the City Council concerning the Riverside County
Integrated Plan ("County Plan")

These provisions do not disqualify an official from holding an elected or appointed public
office. Other laws, however, may apply.
2. No. Generally, Councilmember Buckley will not have conflict of interest
disqualifizing him from being involved in City Council decisions which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on clients of O'Reilly, since those clients
will not be considered sources of income to him. (Section 82030(a).) However, if a City
Council decision involving one or more of those clients will also have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on O'Reilly, he will have a potentially disqualiffing
conflict of interest.
3. It appears that Councilmember Buckley will have a conflict of interest
potentially disquali$ing him from participating in City Council decisions concerning the
lawsuit with the Water District; it appears reasonably foreseeable that that these decisions
may have a material financial effect upon O'Rellly, a source of income to

4. & 5. No. It does not appear that Councilmember Buckley will have a conflict
of interest potentially disqualifuing him from participating in City Council decisions
concerning City funding and prqect scheduling for the Watershed Authority, and City
Council decisions involving the County Plan. Based on your facts, it does not appear
reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material financial effect upon
O'Reilly.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from makin g, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. Section 87100 does not prohibit a public official from
holding a position in an agency, but describes those circumstances in which a public
official may not be involved in the governmental decisions of his or her agency.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifiiing conflict of interest (regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(l) -
(8)), which is discussed below. The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest
may occur whenever a public official makes, participates in making, or influences a
govemmental decision which may materially affect one or more of his or her economic
interests.
l'. & 2. rs councilmember Buckley a public official making, participating in
making or influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As an elected
member of the City of Lake Elsinore City Council, he is a public official. (Section 82048;
regulation 18701(a).) As a council member, unless disqualified under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act, he will make, participate in making, and influence the
governmental decisions in question. (Section 87100; regulations 18702.1 - 15702.3.)
3. What are Councilmember Buckley's economic interests?
The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined in
regulations 18703-18703.5. Under the facts provided, Councilmember Buckley's
potential economic interests, which might give rise to a conflict of interest, are The
Buckley Company, O'Reilly, and other sources of income to The Buckley Company
aggregating $500 or more over the twelve-month period preceding his making,
participating in making, or influencing govemmental decisions in the matters discussed
above.a
The Buckley Company
The Buckley Company is an economic interest to him, as a business entity for
which he is the sole owner. (Regulation 18703.1(b).) Although you do not state the
value of his direct investment in his business, if this value is $2,000 or more, his direct
investment (as a matter independent of his ownership interest) would be yet another basis
upon which to identiff The Buckley Company as a business entity that is an economic
interest to him. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.1(a).) Finally, he also has an economic
interest in The Buckley Company as a source of income to him that aggregates to $500 or
more over a twelve-month period prior to the date that he will be called upon to make,
participate in making, or influence the govemmental decisions you describe. (Section
871 03(c); regulation 1 8703.3.)
O'Reilly
O'Reilly, as his former employer during the past twelve months, is also an
economic interest to Councilmember Buckley since it is a source of income to him
personally, aggregating $500 or more over the prior twelve months.s
Moreover, in some circumstances, a public official may also have an economic
interest in clients or other sources of income to a business entity in which he or she has an
ownership interest. (Section 82030(a).) The most corlmon case is when the public
official's ownership interest in the business entity is lO-percent or greater. (/d.) Since
Councilmember Buckley's ownership interest in The Buckley Company is 1O-percent or
greater, clients such as O'Reilly and other sources of income to The Buckley Company

has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision

official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period.
(Regulations 1 8703.5 and 18705.5.)
t Whe., a source of income is a former employer the former employer is not considered a source of
income to the public official if all of the following apply: l) all income from the employer was received by
or accrued to the public official prior to the time he or she became a public official; 2) the income was
received in the normal course of the previous employment; and 3) there was no expectation by the public
official at the time he or she assumed office of renewed employment with the former employer. (Regulation
18703.3(b).) In a telephone conversation with the Commission's staff on November 5,2002, you indicated
that Councilmember Buckley was sworn into office on November 27,2001. Since his employment with
O'Reilly did not terminate until January 2002, this exception is not applicable.
aggregating $500 or more over a relevant twelve-month period are also economic
interests to him under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Murad Advice
Letter, No. I-02-100.)
Riverside County, Water District, Watershed Authority
Since neither Councilmember Buckley nor The Buckley Company have an
ownership interest in O'Reilly, clients of O'Reilly who are to O'Reilly sources of income
are not considered to be sources of income to Councilmember Buckley or The Buckley
Company.6
4. Will these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A person, including a source of income, in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if that person, either
directly or by an agent, initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or
similar request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation l870a.l@)(1) - (a)(2).)
A person is the subject of a proceeding conceming the decision before the agency if the
"decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with," that person. (Regulation
l87oa.1@)(2).) If a person who is an economic interest to a public official is not directly
involved in a governmental decision, the person is deemed to be indirectly involved.
(Regulation 187 04.1 (b).)
The Buckley Company and O'Reilly are not the initiators or subjects of or named
parties in, or otherwise meet the criteria to be considered directly involved in City
Council decisions concerning the Water District lawsuit, funding and scheduling of
projects for the Watershed Authority, and decisions involving the County Plan. The two
companies, therefore, are deemed to be indirectly involved in City Council decisions
concerning these matters.
5. Wilt the financial affect on one or more of Councilmember Buckley's economic
interests be material?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
goverTrmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material . (Regulation
18700(a).) For indirectly involved business entities such as The Buckley Company, and
indirectly involved sources of income such as the O'Reilly (and other clients of The
Buckley Company) which are not publicly haded or eligible to be publicly traded, and
are of a small financial size, the financial effect of a governmental decision is material if
the decision will result in:
6In addition, these local govemment entities that are clients of, and sources of income to, O'Reilly
are not parents or subsidiaries of, nor business entities otherwise related to, O'Reilly. Thus, they would not
be economic interests to Councilmember Buckley due to a business affiliation. (Regulation 18703.1(c).)
(a) an increase or decrease in the business entity's or source of income's gross
revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more;
(b) the business entity or source of income incurring or avoiding additional
expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or
(c) an increase or decrease in the value of the business entity's or source of
income's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a)(A)-(c).)
This is afactual question and there are no facts before us to indicate whether an
anticipated financial effect would be material, under the standards above. Ultimately,
this is a question for Councilmember Buckley, and not us, to decide. The Commission
does not act as a finder of fact when it offers advice. (Regulation l13z9(b)(7).)
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that these governmental decisions wiII result in one or
more of the materiality standards being met?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the decision has a reasonably
foreseeable impact on a public official's economic interests that is material . (Regulation
18700(a).) A material financial effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably
foreseeable if there is a substantial probability, under the totality of the circumstances,
that it will occur. (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner, supra fciting t].5. v. Mtsstssippi
Valley Generattng Co.,364 U.S. 520, 555 (1961)1.) In this instance, the circumstances
are comparable to several of the scenarios examined in In re Thorner and lead to similar
advice regarding the reasonable foreseeability of a financial effect.
Water District
Settlement of the litigation between the City and the Water District, as currently
negotiated, would involve permanent use of recycled water to supplement natural flows
of river water into Lake Elsinore. In the past, the Water District has implemented interim
flows of recycled water into Lake Elsinore and in conjunction with such activity,
sponsored a public awareness campaign concerning use of recycled water in Lake
Elsinore. The Water District retained O'Reilly to conduct that campaign. You state that
if the Water District decides to sponsor a future public awareness campaign concerning
use of recycled water, it is anticipated that O'Reilly will conduct that campaign as well.
This is analogous to a situation discussed in In re Thorner, in.which the past
business dealings of the parties created a substantial probability that a particular agency
decision would have a material financial effect upon the parties, even in the absence of a
current formal agreement or understanding between the parties. Here, the past linkage
between the Water District's introduction of re-cycled water into Lake Elsinore and its
sponsorship of a public awareness campaign concerning use of re-cycled water, together
with hiring O'Reilly to conduct that campaign, creates a substantial probability that a
settlement affecting permanent use of re-cycled water in Lake Elsinore will similarly
result in a public awareness campaign sponsored by the Water District, and that O'Reilly
will conduct that campaign. Indeed, you concede in your letter that if such a campaign is
conducted, it is anticipated that O'Reilly will conduct that campaign. Thus, unless
Councilmember Buckley is able to acquire facts demonstrating that the Water District
will not conduct this public awareness campaign, it appears reasonably foreseeable that
O'Reilly will be financially affectedT by a City Council decision regarding settlement of
the lawsuit. Whether the financial effect will be material as discussed under Step 5 above
is a factual question the Councilmember must ultimately decide.
Waters hed Authority/Riverstde County
In re Thorner also addressed a sifuation where aparly currently doing business
with a public official has submitted, or will submit, a bid on a project allowed by the
official's govefilmental decision. Unless thatparty is awarded a contract for that project,
there is an extra degree of remoteness added to the foreseeability of a financial effect
flowing from the earlier decision; the contract has to be awarded and the party has to
fiilize his existing business arrangement with the official to provide services called for
under the contract before the decision would have a financial effect. The Commission
advised that in such context, a financial effect was not reasonably foreseeable.
This is analogous to the upcoming City Council decisions regarding the
Watershed Authority and Riverside County. Both entities have current contracts with
O'Reilly to perform government, media or community relations services. However, you
provide no facts indicating that the upcoming decisions, which concern funding and
prioritizing ofprojects for the Watershed Authority, and Riverside County's County Plan,
have any relationship to whether these entities might seek additional governmental,
media, or community relations services from O'Reilly. Once those decisions are made,
the Watershed Authority and Riverside County would then have to not only implement
these decisions, but also (1) undertake some form of governmental, media, or community
relations effort as part of that implementation, and (2) ttilize their existing contracts with
O'Reilly to do so, before the City Council decisions would have a financial effect on
O'Reilly. This demonstrates that extra degree of remoteness spoken of in In re Thorner
as characteizing a decision that will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect.
Thus, it does not appear reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material
financial effect upon O'Reilly.
7. & 8. Public Generallyllegally Required Participation
We have not gone on to analyze these last two steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies when the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the public
official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
Step eight is an exception that applies when the public official is legally required to
participate in the decision. You have not provided any facts to suggest that these
exceptions apply.
' we disc.rss further below whether that financial effect is to be deemed material.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifying him
from serving as the City's representative on the Riverside County Transportation
commission ("Transportation Commission"), the western Riverside Council of
Govemments ('tylBgG"), of various subcommittees of these entities?
2. Generally, does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest
disqualiffing him from being involved in City Council decisions which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on clients of O'Reilly Public Relations
("O'Reilly"), a media consulting company?
3. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifring him
from being involved in decisions of the Lake Elsinore City Council ("City Council")
concerning a pending lawsuit, and settlement of that suit, between the City of Lake
Elsinore (the "City") and the Elsinore Valley Municipal Water District ("Water
District")?
4. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualiffing him
being involved in decisions of the City Council concerning City funding for the Lake
Elsinore & San Jacinto Watershed Authority ("Watershed Authority'), the prioritizing of
funding and scheduling of projects for the Watershed Authority, and involving the
Watershed Authority as aparty to the Water District settlement?
5. Does Councilmember Buckley have a conflict of interest disqualifying him
from being involved in decisions of the City Council concerning the Riverside County
Integrated Plan ("County Plan")

CONCLUSION: 1. No. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to a public
official when making, participating in making, or influencing agovernmental decision.
These provisions do not disqualify an official from holding an elected or appointed public
office. Other laws, however, may apply.
2. No. Generally, Councilmember Buckley will not have conflict of interest
disqualifizing him from being involved in City Council decisions which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on clients of O'Reilly, since those clients
will not be considered sources of income to him. (Section 82030(a).) However, if a City
Council decision involving one or more of those clients will also have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on O'Reilly, he will have a potentially disqualiffing
conflict of interest.
3. It appears that Councilmember Buckley will have a conflict of interest
potentially disquali$ing him from participating in City Council decisions concerning the
lawsuit with the Water District; it appears reasonably foreseeable that that these decisions
may have a material financial effect upon O'Rellly, a source of income to
Councilmember Buckley. This is a factual question for Councilmember Buckley to
ultimately decide.
4. & 5. No. It does not appear that Councilmember Buckley will have a conflict
of interest potentially disqualifuing him from participating in City Council decisions
concerning City funding and prqect scheduling for the Watershed Authority, and City
Council decisions involving the County Plan. Based on your facts, it does not appear
reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material financial effect upon
O'Reilly.
